title: Fix TLS 1.2 side-by-side: last step unreachable due to shared stepIndex
description: NetworkingModule.tsx:505-548 — TLS 1.3 has 8 messages, TLS 1.2 has 9, but both SequenceDiagram instances receive the same stepIndex. When stepIndex=7 (last TLS 1.3 step), TLS 1.2's ApplicationData (step 9) is unreachable. The properties panel step counter only counts TLS 1.3 messages. Fix: use Math.max(tls13.length, tls12.length) for totalSteps in side-by-side mode.
acceptanceCriteria: In TLS 1.2 comparison mode, all TLS 1.2 steps are reachable
Step counter shows correct total for the longer of the two
Both diagrams animate correctly at every stepIndex

title: Fix DNS visualization: cache hits never visible despite legend showing them
description: NetworkingModule.tsx:568-582 — DNSVisualization resolves twice (cold + warm), but renders first-pass events only (line 578: allEvents.slice(0, firstPassLength)) where cache is empty. The green 'Cache Hit' legend appears (line 601-604) but green rows NEVER appear because first-pass events never have cached:true. The legend lies to the student. Fix: add a toggle between cold-cache (first resolution) and warm-cache (second resolution) views.
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle button switches between 'First Lookup (Cold Cache)' and 'Cached Lookup (Warm Cache)'
In warm-cache mode, green Cache Hit rows actually appear
Legend accurately reflects what colors are present in each mode

title: Fix CORS SIMPLE_CONTENT_TYPES defined but never used — POST application/json skips preflight
description: cors-simulator.ts:98-102 — SIMPLE_CONTENT_TYPES set is defined (application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, text/plain) but never referenced in the preflight logic at lines 204-208. A POST with Content-Type: application/json and no custom headers incorrectly classified as simple, skipping preflight. Per the Fetch spec, non-simple Content-Types MUST trigger preflight. Fix: add Content-Type value checking to the simple-request determination.
acceptanceCriteria: POST with Content-Type: application/json triggers preflight
POST with Content-Type: text/plain does NOT trigger preflight
SIMPLE_CONTENT_TYPES set is actually used in the logic
Step description explains why Content-Type triggers preflight

title: Fix TCP TIME_WAIT state silently overwritten — students never see it in timeline
description: tcp-state-machine.ts:482-509 — TIME_WAIT is set at line 483 and recorded in the event at line 489. But at lines 505-508, the state is immediately overwritten to CLOSED with no event. The description at line 499 says 'enters TIME_WAIT (waits 2*MSL before CLOSED)' but the code instantly transitions past it. Students never observe the TIME_WAIT → CLOSED transition. Fix: either add a 5th teardown event for the transition, or leave the final state as TIME_WAIT with documentation.
acceptanceCriteria: TIME_WAIT state is observable in the event timeline
A separate event or annotation shows TIME_WAIT → CLOSED transition
Description explains why 2*MSL wait exists (prevent old duplicate packets)

title: Fix TLS 1.3 0-RTT color inconsistency — red background on encrypted message
description: tls13-handshake.ts:309,322 — The 0-RTT ApplicationData at tick 2 has encrypted:true but rttPhase:'initial'. The tls13RowBackground function at line 442 colors by encrypted field (green=encrypted, red=plaintext). But the 0-RTT early data IS encrypted with PSK-derived keys, yet its rttPhase is 'initial' which groups it with the plaintext ClientHello. If rowBgFn uses rttPhase instead of encrypted field, the early data would show red. Fix: ensure the color function checks the encrypted field directly, and that 0-RTT data gets its own distinct amber color.
acceptanceCriteria: 0-RTT ApplicationData shows amber background (not red plaintext or green encrypted)
Legend includes '0-RTT (early data)' with amber swatch in all 0-RTT views
Color truthfully represents the security properties (encrypted but not forward-secret)

title: Fix DNS CNAME following emits misleading client→resolver event
description: dns-resolution.ts:341-342 — When cnameDepth > 0 (following a CNAME chain), the event has from:'client' and to:'recursive-resolver'. But the client did NOT send this query — the recursive resolver is following the CNAME internally. The sequence diagram shows a false arrow from Client to Resolver. Fix: when cnameDepth > 0, set from:'recursive-resolver' to:'recursive-resolver' (internal processing step).
acceptanceCriteria: CNAME-following steps show as internal resolver processing, not client queries
Sequence diagram shows self-arrow on Recursive column for CNAME following
First query (depth 0) still correctly shows Client → Recursive

title: Replace traffic-light color scheme in HTTP and API comparisons with neutral colors
description: NetworkingModule.tsx:665,673,681 — HTTP/1.1=red, HTTP/2=amber, HTTP/3=green embeds a false value judgment (red=bad, green=good). Line 1316-1319 — REST=red, gRPC=green does the same. HTTP/1.1 is not 'bad' and REST is often the right choice. Fix: use neutral distinguishing colors (e.g., blue/purple/teal for HTTP versions, blue/pink/teal for APIs) or add a prominent note that color is for identification only.
acceptanceCriteria: HTTP version colors are distinguishable but not value-loaded
API protocol colors are distinguishable but not value-loaded
No traffic-light red/amber/green pattern in comparison visualizations

title: Fix CDN cache-miss and WebSocket close colored red — implies error for normal operations
description: cdn-flow.ts:202 — cache-miss colored red (#ef4444) but first request MUST miss. Lines 213-217 — purge colored red but purging is deliberate. NetworkingModule.tsx:883 — graceful WebSocket close (status 1000) colored red. Fix: use amber/orange for cache-miss (expected/neutral), distinct non-red color for purge (operational), gray/neutral for graceful WS close. Reserve red only for actual errors.
acceptanceCriteria: Cache-miss uses amber/orange, not red
Cache-purge uses a distinct operational color (not red)
WebSocket graceful close (1000) uses gray/neutral, not red
Red reserved for actual error conditions only

title: Fix DNS/CDN/ConnectionPool legend gaps — missing states that appear in visualization
description: DNS (NetworkingModule.tsx:594-607): missing 'Query' state (rows with no background). CDN (lines 1162-1181): missing 'Response' (blue). ConnectionPool (ConnectionPoolVisualization.tsx:444-456): missing 'Destroyed' and 'Draining' (2 of 5 states). Fix: add all missing states to their respective legends.
acceptanceCriteria: DNS legend includes 'Query' entry (or gives queries a light background)
CDN legend includes 'Response' with blue swatch
ConnectionPool legend includes all 5 states from statusColor()

title: Eliminate DNS data divergence between canvas and properties panel
description: NetworkingModule.tsx:568-582 vs 2390-2393 — DNSVisualization creates its own DNSResolver and resolves twice. useNetworkingModule creates a separate resolver and resolves once. Two independent instances with independent tick counters. Fix: compute DNS events in one place (the hook) and pass them as props to DNSVisualization.
acceptanceCriteria: Single DNSResolver instance per scenario
Canvas and properties panel show identical step data
Tick numbers in step descriptions match between panels

title: Add WebSocket heartbeat initiator parameter (currently hardcoded server→client)
description: websocket-lifecycle.ts:226 — heartbeat() always sends Ping from server. Per RFC 6455 §5.5.2, either endpoint can initiate Ping. Students incorrectly learn only servers send Pings. Fix: add optional initiator parameter defaulting to 'server'. Show both directions in the simulation.
acceptanceCriteria: heartbeat() accepts optional initiator: 'client' | 'server' parameter
Default behavior unchanged (server sends Ping)
Visualization can show client-initiated Ping scenario

title: Add TCP state-transition row coloring and RTT brackets (match TLS visual richness)
description: NetworkingModule.tsx:2694-2703 — TCP uses plain SequenceDiagram with no row backgrounds, no RTT brackets, no state coloring. Compared to TLS (plaintext/encrypted colors, RTT brackets), TCP is visually sparse. Fix: add state-based row coloring (SYN=amber, ESTABLISHED=green, FIN=red) and a '1.5 RTT' bracket for the handshake. Bring TCP to the same visual richness as TLS.
acceptanceCriteria: TCP handshake steps have amber row background (transitional)
ESTABLISHED data transfer steps have green row background
Teardown steps have red/amber row background
RTT bracket shows '1 RTT' for the handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)

title: Enrich WebSocket visualization — add upgrade headers, frame format, message counter
description: WebSocket has only 3 representation types (lowest in module). Missing: HTTP upgrade headers (Connection, Upgrade, Sec-WebSocket-Key), frame opcode bytes visualization, message counter metrics. TLS has 6 representations. Fix: add upgrade header detail panel, show frame format (FIN|RSV|opcode|mask|length), add bidirectional message counter.
acceptanceCriteria: Upgrade request/response HTTP headers visible in properties or bottom panel
Frame format visualization shows opcode, mask, length fields
Live message counter shows messages sent in each direction
At least 5 representation types (up from 3)

title: Fix serialization feature matrix hardcoded arrays — will silently break if format order changes
description: NetworkingModule.tsx:1794,1809,1821 — Schema Evolution, Browser Native, and Streaming Support are hardcoded as literal arrays (['limited','excellent','limited','excellent'], [true,false,false,false], [false,true,false,true]). These rely on results maintaining JSON/Protobuf/MsgPack/Avro order. Fix: move these properties into SerializationResult interface alongside humanReadable and schemaRequired.
acceptanceCriteria: SerializationResult has schemaEvolution, browserNative, streamingSupport fields
Feature matrix reads from result objects, not hardcoded arrays
Adding/reordering formats does not break the matrix

title: Add 'every byte explained' hex view toggle for TCP and TLS (inspired by tls13.xargs.org)
description: Research shows tls13.xargs.org's clickable hex dump is the gold standard for protocol teaching. ByteLens.dev does similar for TCP/UDP/DNS. Add a toggle to TCP and TLS visualizations that shows the actual byte layout of each message. Clicking a hex region expands to show field name, size, and purpose. Uses existing HeaderField data from protocol-deep-dive.ts.
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle button switches between sequence diagram and hex/byte view
Hex view shows protocol header fields color-coded by purpose
Clicking a field region shows name, size, purpose, and example value
Uses HeaderField data from protocol-deep-dive.ts for TCP and TLS

title: Add adjustable RTT and packet-loss sliders to TCP and HTTP comparison
description: Research shows toolkit.whysonil.dev's adjustable parameters (RTT, packet loss) turn passive viewers into active explorers. Add sliders to TCP (adjust RTT, inject packet loss percentage) and HTTP comparison (adjust latency, show impact on HOL blocking). Students can experiment: 'what happens with 200ms RTT and 5% loss?'
acceptanceCriteria: TCP visualization has RTT slider (10ms-500ms) and packet loss slider (0-50%)
HTTP comparison has latency slider affecting all three version timings
Changing parameters immediately recalculates and re-renders the visualization
Step descriptions update to reflect custom parameters

title: Add image tile race demo for HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 multiplexing visualization
description: Research shows imagekit.io/demo/http2-vs-http1 (100 image tiles loading side-by-side) is the single most persuasive visualization in networking education. Add a simulated version: split an image into N tiles, show HTTP/1.1 loading 6-at-a-time vs HTTP/2 loading all simultaneously. The visual difference is undeniable and creates an instant 'aha' moment.
acceptanceCriteria: Grid of 30+ tiles shown for HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 side-by-side
HTTP/1.1 loads tiles in batches of 6 (connection limit visible)
HTTP/2 loads all tiles simultaneously (multiplexing visible)
Adjustable tile count and simulated network conditions

title: Fix PacketJourney response direction using fragile hardcoded index threshold
description: PacketJourneySimulator.tsx:162 — `isResponse = hopIdx >= hops.length - 3` hardcodes that last 3 hops are response direction. Works for current hop configs but breaks if hops added/removed. Fix: add direction:'request'|'response' field to HopStage interface directly.
acceptanceCriteria: HopStage has a direction field
Direction logic reads from hop data, not index arithmetic
Adding/removing hops does not break direction detection

title: Label serialization hex dumps as simulated — students may try to decode fake bytes
description: serialization-comparison.ts:176-208 — simulateHexDump generates pseudo-random bytes that look realistic but are completely fake. A student who tries to decode the hex will get nonsense. The serialized field shows this data with no disclaimer. Fix: prepend '// SIMULATED binary representation (not actual encoding)\n' to each hex dump. Or better, use real encoding libraries.
acceptanceCriteria: Each binary format's serialized output starts with a clear 'SIMULATED' label
UI shows a note that binary representations are approximations
JSON output (which IS real) is not labeled as simulated

title: Consolidate TLS 1.3 data — tls13-handshake.ts and tls-handshake.ts both model the same handshake
description: tls13-handshake.ts (static const arrays) and tls-handshake.ts:104-237 (class method) both model TLS 1.3 1-RTT. Neither references the other. Description differences between the two could confuse students. Fix: make tls13-handshake.ts the canonical data source and have TLSHandshake.performHandshake() return data derived from the same const arrays.
acceptanceCriteria: Single source of truth for TLS 1.3 1-RTT message data
Both code paths produce identical step descriptions
No duplication of protocol message content

title: Add TCP empty-string guard in sendData to prevent 0-byte data segments
description: tcp-state-machine.ts:272-341 — sendData('') produces a 0-byte data segment where sequence number doesn't advance and receiver ACKs with same number. Real TCP wouldn't send a 0-byte PSH segment. Fix: throw an error for empty data strings.
acceptanceCriteria: sendData('') throws descriptive error
sendData with non-empty string works unchanged
Error message explains why empty data is invalid
